channel.title: Self on Fengyu WANG
channel.description: Recent content in Self on Fengyu WANG

item.title: Why Low-End Customers Keep Finding You
item.description:
If a product or service consistently attracts low-quality customers with limited budgets, the problem is usually not the customers. It is the way the product is presented.

The market has an invisible screening mechanism: the threshold you set determines the people you attract. The threshold is not just price. It also includes your tone of voice, your presentation style, your communication manner, and your transaction process.

When you set your price too low, you are effectively telling the market that your value is low. When your description is full of words like cheap, discount, help, and guide, you attract customers looking for a bargain, not customers looking for value. When you present yourself as flexible, negotiable, and always willing to concede, the signal you send is that your service is negotiable.

item.title: Workplace Survival to Entrepreneurship
item.description:
The transition from workplace survival to independent entrepreneurship is essentially an upgrade in cognition. What most people accumulate at work is the ability to do a job well, not the ability to run a business. These two require completely different skill structures.

The workplace survival phase is about building professional competence and reputation. You need to become more professional than most in one or two areas, so that the company feels it is worth keeping you. The strategy for this phase can be summarised as: use professional competence to earn job security, use professional reputation to earn room for growth.

item.title: First Principles of Making Money
item.description:
The first principle of making money is creating value. Following this logic chain reveals a complete framework: creating value requires solving problems, solving problems requires identifying real needs through practice, iterating fast, validating models, and building systems that scale.

The first principle of making money is creating value, not selling time. The first principle of creating value is solving problems, not providing cheap labour. The first principle of solving problems is identifying real needs, not self-indulgence. The first principle of identifying real needs is practical feedback, not working in isolation. The first principle of practical feedback is fast iteration, not polishing to perfection. The first principle of fast iteration is validating the model, not making deliveries. The first principle of validating the model is building for scale, not grinding alone. The first principle of scaling is leverage thinking, not linear accumulation.

item.title: Surface-Level Experience vs. Core Capabilities
item.description:
In the workplace, capability management can be strategically divided into two layers: surface-level experience and core capabilities. Use the surface layer to build trust, and protect the core layer as your competitive moat.

The first step is capabilitylayering. Separate what you can share from what you must never share. Surface-level experience includes general skills, industrycommon knowledge, publicly shareable methodologies, and commonwork experience. These can be freely shared. They help you build a professional image and earn the trust of colleagues and managers.

item.title: Execution Without Business Sense
item.description:
Many people fail in business not because they lack execution, but because they have execution without business sense. The stronger the execution, the faster they lose money when heading in the wrong direction.

The portrait of this type of person isexceptionally clear. They act immediately, with extraordinary execution. On a whim, they rent a shop, renovate it, stock it, and open for business, at a speed that is genuinely impressive. But they have no idea how to count. How to sell, how to manage people, how to control costs, how to calculate profit. They do not know and do not care. The result is that they only know how to pay, not how to collect. They only know how to work, not how to make money.

item.title: The PDCA Cycle
item.description:
There is a problem-solving method that has been repeatedly validated in practice. First study the known methods for solving this type of problem. Then try one. Observe the results. If it does not work, adjust the approach and try again. Keep iterating until the problem is solved.

This process has corresponding theoretical frameworks in multiple academic fields. The most classic is the PDCA cycle: Plan, Do, Check, Act. The process described above is exactly a standard PDCA closed loop: researching methods is Plan, trying them is Do, identifying problems is Check, adjusting the approach is Act.

item.title: Stay Honest in a World That Prefers You Did Not
item.description:
Staying honest and clear-headed at work often earns you a strange label: being told you have low EQ, that you are too rigid. This feedback can make you doubt yourself, but the truth it reveals is exactly the opposite.

The people calling you low EQ are exactly the ones whose path to muddy waters you are blocking.

Break it down: when they say you have low EQ, what they really mean is you are too honest, too principled, too hard to fool, too hard to drag into their shady dealings.

item.title: Being Rational, Objective, and Honest
item.description:
There is a structural contradiction between offering rational, honest consulting services and making easy money. Honesty itself is a sales barrier because the truth is often not what clients want to hear.

Observing the businesses that make money quickly, they typically share a common feature: they exploit information asymmetry and human weakness. This is not a moral judgement. It is a factual observation of how the market operates.

For those who choose the path of rationality, objectivity, and honesty, the dilemma is real. Honesty is itself a sales obstacle. Because being honest means telling clients things they do not want to hear: there are no shortcuts, it takes time, there are risks. These truths benefit the client in the long run, but in the short-term game, the person who tells the truth loses to the person who tells the client what they want to hear.

item.title: Fake It Till You Make It: Taking the First Step
item.description:
The logic of taking the first step runs counter to most people instinct. The first step is never about being ready.

You do not need to be highly professional, highly skilled, deeply knowledgeable, or vastly experienced. The first step requires only one thing: put it out there. List it on Xianyu. Your service is live. You are now in business.

The reality behind this logic is that the first step is not about demonstrating capability. It is about claiming a position. You do not need to be qualified before you enter the arena. You just need to show up first. Many doctors are not fully proficient when they start their jobs. They only truly learn after they have started.

item.title: Separating Business and Personal Accounts
item.description:
Running a business and creating content are not in conflict. In fact, they complement each other. This is not an either-or choice. It is a question of resource allocation.

From a logical perspective, these two paths not only avoid conflict, they reinforce each other.

Enterprise services target the B2B market: building software, solving problems for companies, earning money through professionalism and reliability, with stable income, high customer value, and strong repeat business. Content creation targets the C2C market: making videos, writing opinions, building a personal brand, earning through influence and reach.

item.title: Time Moves Fast and Slow at the Same Time
item.description:
Nearly every office worker experiences this split perception of time: the day flies by, yet every minute feels like it is dragging. There is a clear psychological mechanism behind this feeling.

From a psychological perspective, these two feelings have completely different causes.

Time feels fast when your brain is not truly processing anything. Meetings, replying to messages, filling forms, busywork, scrolling on your phone. None of these require deep thinking. Your brain is on autopilot. No memory anchors, so the day passes as if you have not lived it. Psychologists call this time numbness: repetitive, mechanical, thoughtless time passes fastest in our perception.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
My work is built on a core belief: every complex problem can be broken down through a clear logical framework, analysed efficiently with the help of artificial intelligence, and turned into systematic solutions that are reusable, replicable, and actionable.

I offer decision planning and problem-solving services to individuals, entrepreneurs, and businesses, helping clients establish clear judgement in complex environments, reduce risk, optimise pathways, improve efficiency, and achieve long-term goals.

My service areas cover business development, marketing operations, media and communications, software applications, career development, life planning, and resource allocation.
